svg.title: Redis Atomicity: Lua vs MULTI/EXEC vs Bare Commands
svg.desc: Deep dive into how Redis handles concurrent commands, why Lua scripts are atomic, and when to use each approach.
svg.text: Redis Atomicity — Why Lua, Not MULTI/EXEC
Redis processes commands one at a time on a single thread. No parallelism, no locking, no mutexes.
But "single-threaded" doesn't mean "atomic across multiple commands." Between YOUR two commands,
another client's command can slip in. The event loop interleaves commands from different connections.
THE RACE: TWO CLIENTS SENDING INCR + EXPIRE
Client A: INCR returns 1 → about to EXPIRE → process dies
Client B: INCR returns 2 → EXPIRE (but A already set it?)
Result: key has count=2 but NO TTL → phone rate-limited forever
Window: microseconds. Consequence: permanent. Probability: low but nonzero.
In production at 1000 req/s, "low probability" events happen daily.
All commands queued, executed together, no interleaving.
But you can't READ the INCR result mid-transaction.
So EXPIRE runs on EVERY call, not just count===1.
4 out of 5 EXPIREs are unnecessary (key already has TTL).
1. Receives EVAL command with the script
2. Blocks ALL other clients (event loop paused)
3. Runs INCR → reads result → conditionally EXPIREs
4. Unblocks event loop → returns count to caller
No other command from any client can execute during 2-4.
Round trips: 1 (entire script is one call)
Our Lua script: 2 Redis commands, ~5 microseconds execution. Redis handles ~100,000 commands/sec.
Blocking for 5μs means: 1 client waits 5μs. At 1000 req/s, total blocking = 5ms/sec = 0.5% of Redis capacity.
When Lua becomes dangerous: scanning 100K keys, complex string manipulation, loops over large datasets.
Rule of thumb: if your script touches ≤5 keys and has no loops, it's free. If it scans or loops, use EVALSHA
(cached compiled script — skips parse overhead) or redesign to avoid the script entirely.
